coredata.dc:title: Study of the electron–photon interaction on the spin-dependent transport in nano-structures
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Modarresi, M.
Mogulkoc, A.
Roknabadi, M.R.
Behdani, M.
coredata.dc:description: Transport through a nano device in the presence of light illumination has been studied by using unitary-transformation scheme together with the non-equilibrium Green's function formalism. The non-interacting Hamiltonian is written within the framework of the tight binding model and structureless electrodes are described in wide-band approximation. The interaction between electrons is modeled by the mean-field Hubbard term. Our results indicate that we can tune the "intrinsic magnetization of zigzag edge" and "spin-polarized current" by using light illumination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Hubbard model
Electron–photon interaction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic structure of T-GQD between two semi-infinite electrodes in the presence of light illumination. (b) Different bonds between carbon atoms.
The electronic eigenstates of T-GQD in the presence of electron–photon interaction for N=7, 9 and 11.
The transmission as a function of electron energy for N=7 and 15 with different value of α.
The normalized photocurrent as a function of applied bias voltage for N=7–15.
The variation of spin-polarization in the presence of incident light.
The normalized photocurrent as a function of photon wavelength.
The critical value of α for different structures.

head.ce:title: Study of the electron–photon interaction on the spin-dependent transport in nano-structures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ankara University, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transport through a nano device in the presence of light illumination has been studied by using unitary-transformation scheme together with the non-equilibrium Green's function formalism. The non-interacting Hamiltonian is written within the framework of the tight binding model and structureless electrodes are described in wide-band approximation. The interaction between electrons is modeled by the mean-field Hubbard term. Our results indicate that we can tune the "intrinsic magnetization of zigzag edge" and "spin-polarized current" by using light illumination.
We studied the transport through a graphene quantum dot in the presence of light illumination.

ce:section.ce:para: The nano science is about the investigation of structure in the molecular scale. The electronic transport through a nano scale device sandwiched between two leads is an interesting topic for both experimental [1–4] and theoretical physicists [5–13]. The non-equilibrium Green's function formalism is an appropriate model for investigation such a transport trough a nano device [5–7,9–13]. In the presence of different many body interactions the transport becomes a sophisticated problem from the theoretical point of view. The electron–electron interactions are very important in the transport properties of nano-devices which are responsible for the Coulomb blockade and the Kondo effect [9,14,15]. The coupling between the fermionic and bosonic systems of such a device is very interesting. Due to the very long spin relaxation and de-coherence time graphene is an appropriate material for future spintronic devices [16,17]. Most of the recent works based on the mean-field theory or configuration interaction (CI) method predict the net magnetic moments in the zigzag edges of graphene quantum dots due to the sub-lattice symmetry breaking [18–24]. In this work, a typical triangle graphene quantum dot (T-GQD) with zigzag edges between two infinite electrodes in the presence of light illumination has been considered. We investigate the electron–photon interaction in a typical nano scale device which is an applicable model for photo-detector devices. The electron–electron interactions are modeled by using the mean-field Hubbard Hamiltonian. The dark self-energy which is the self-energy in the dark condition contains the effect of left and right electrodes on the T-GQD. Our main goal is to investigate the effect of the electron–photon interaction on the magnetic and spin-dependent transport properties of nano device considered here. The theoretical investigation of electron–photon interaction provide a better understanding of underlying physics to design more efficient nano-photonic devices. Here, the main problem comes from the coupling between the fermion and boson system. We present two successive methods for considering the coupled fermion–boson system which was used in previous works [25,26]. While the first method is based on unitary transformation which estimates the contribution of whole independent boson system, the other one is the non-equilibrium Green's function to calculate the transport through the nano structure.

The pure electronic Hamiltonian of the T-GQD contains the non-interacting and the Hubbard term
(1)
H
T − G Q D

where
ε
i
is the on-site energy of carbon atoms and
t
i , j
is the hopping between nearest neighbor atoms.
C
i , σ
†
(
C
i , σ
) is the electron creation (annihilation) operator for an electron with spin σ at site i. The last term in the Eq. (1) describes the interaction between electrons with opposite spin on the same site. The strength of interaction, U will be considered as a parameter in our calculations. The Hubbard Hamiltonian can be discussed in the mean-field sense [20,22–24] and Configuration–Interaction (CI) [19,21,22] methods. Here we have used the mean field approach which has been discussed in literatures. In this work, our main concern is to focus the effect of electron–photon interaction term on the transport properties of nano device.

In the next step, we consider a T-GQD between two semi-infinite leads as left and right electrodes in the presence of electron–photon interaction as shown in Fig. 1.a for a dot with N atoms in the zigzag edge.

The matrix form of the total Hamiltonian of the above described system in the presence of electron photon interaction can be written as,
(2)
H =

where H
L/R and H
L/R,GQD are Hamiltonians of left/Right electrodes and the coupling between electrodes and T-GQD, respectively. Here, the contribution of electrodes in the Hamiltonian of system is modeled by specifying the self-energy of left and right electrodes
Σ
L / R
. For the sake of simplicity, the self-energy of electrodes are written in the simple wide-band approximation. In this approximation the imaginary part of self-energy is considered a constant number and the real-part is ignored. H
e–ph describes the electron–photon interaction in the central region which is zero in the case of dark condition.

where a, b, c and d are atomic positions according to Fig. 1.b. M 0 contains the energy and the number of photon which corresponds to the intensity of incident light. In the presence of electron–photon interaction, the total Hamiltonian of the isolated structure from electrodes is H =
H

. The electron–photon interaction includes also a term ~

which permits the particle to change its state by emitting or absorbing a photon. Independent boson models, such terms make Hamiltonian unsolvable, and in most cases their contributions are omitted [28]. Due to the this difficulty, we present a unitary transformation in the form

to estimate the contribution of the interaction term. The transformation given by Eq. (1) is the well-known displaced oscillator transformation which shifts bosonic coordinates by an amount of the interaction amplitude,
M

In Eq. (8), we only consider the on-site scattering terms that contribute to the Hubbard Hamiltonian, the other scattering terms are ignored. By applying the unitary transformation given by Eq. (7) we reach to an effective Hamiltonian for the GQD in the presence of electron–photon interaction.

The contribution to the on-site energy from the interaction term is zero except for atoms in the zigzag edges. As a result, the interaction removes the degeneracy of energy levels in the Fermi level. The effective Hamiltonian can be solved self-consistently starting from a random electronic density. The spin density on each atomic site is
M
i
= (
n
i ↑
−
n
i ↓
) / 2 and the total spin is S =
∑ i
M
i
. Here, we consider all the atomic positions are the same and
ε
i
= 0 , t = 2.7
eV and U / t = 1.1
. For our calculations, we consider number of photons, photon energy and other constants as an adjustable parameter α (
N
p h
, ℏ ω ) =
(
N
p h
/ 2
ε
0
V )
1 / 2
( e
a
C − C
ℏ ω ) = γ (
N
p h
) / ℏ ω . Fig. 2 shows the eigenstates of T-GQD with different width in the presence electron–photon interaction. In the absence of any kind of interaction in the central region the Fermi level has n-fold degeneracy due to the localized edge states. By considering the interaction of electrons, the degeneracy in the non-interacting states around the Fermi level is lifted. As a result the deference between the occupations of spin up and down states produces a net magnetization in the T-GQD. By applying the external radiation, the degeneracy of electronic levels is affected by the electron–photon interaction. The most important effect is around the Fermi level which removes the electronic degeneracy of the non-interacting states and decreases the magnetization. There are two distinction behavior for GQD with odd and even number of atoms. Our calculations show that there is a threshold for the electron–photon coupling
α
T
, above this value the total magnetization is minimized. The minimum value of magnetization is 1 / 2 and 0 for structures with odd and even number of atoms, respectively. The additional spin-independent interaction with photons screens the effect of electron–electron interaction, and decreases the net magnetization of system. Table 1 shows the value of
α
T
for different triangle GQD. By increasing the number of zigzag edges of T-GQD the interaction becomes more efficient and the critical value of α decreases. Our results show how to tune the finite macroscopic magnetization in a typical graphene dot with zigzag edge by using radiation of a mono-chromatic light. The variation of electronic structure and total net magnetization will affect all the physical quantities. In the next part of this work we will study the transport through T-GQD in the presence of electron–photon interaction.

Generally, in the equilibrium situation current calculated by using the Meir–Wingreen relation which is transformed to the Landauer–Büttiker equation in the case of non-interacting or dark condition [9,24].
(9)

)

where

is the Fermi distribution function in the left/right electrode with the chemical potential μ

/ 2) and Fermi energy ε
f . The
T
D / p h
( ε ) = T r (
Γ

D / p h
r
Γ
R
G
D / p h
a
) is known as the transmission spectrum.
G
σ , D / p h
r / a
is the retarded/advance Green's function in the absence of electron–photon interaction which defined as
(10)
G
σ , D

( ε ) =

σ
−
Σ

]
− 1

The advanced Green's function is the complex-conjugate of retarded Green's function.
Σ
D
r
=
Σ
L
r
+
Σ
R
r
is the retarded dark self-energy which describes system in the absence of electron–photon interaction and includes the retarded self-energy of left and right electrodes. The self-energy of electrodes contains all the effects of electrodes on the total Hamiltonian of nano device. In this approximation, the self-energy operator is written in an energy-independent form as,
Σ
L / R
r
= ( − i / 2 )
Γ
L / R
. An appropriate ansatz
Σ
p h
<
( ε ) = ( Im
Σ
p h
r
( ε ) / Im
Σ
D
r
( ε ) )
Σ
D
<
( ε ) = Λ ( ε )
Σ
D
<
( ε ) leads to a Landauer-like equation for current [9]. The indices D/ph refer to the GQD between two electrodes in the dark/illuminated situation. In the case of weak electron–photon interaction or the strong coupling between GQD and electrodes, Λ ( ε ) → I , and the Landauer formula is valid even in the case of interacting system. But, the electron–photon interaction renormalizes the retarded Green's function of the whole system, and changes all the physical quantities. Fig. 3 shows the transmission spectrum as a function of energy for different structures in the presence of electron–photon-interaction. The transmission peaks are corresponding to the eigen-vlaues of isolated GQD, therefore, the transmission spectrum can be considered as the fingerprint of the electronic structure of system [29]. In the case of N=7 the localized states around the Fermi level do not contribute in the transport through nano-device and there is a relatively large conductance gap. The main effect of the electron–photon interaction is around the Fermi level and above the critical value of α the difference between transmission for spin up and down is minimized. The normalized photo-current
I
P h
/
I
D
= (
I
↑ , P h
+
I
↓ , P h
) / (
I
D
↑
+
I
D
↓
) as a function of bias voltage for different structures and interaction constant is shown in Fig. 4.

The electron–photon interaction is mainly important around the Fermi level, and its effect on levels far from the zero state energy is relatively negligible. By increasing the applied voltage the energy window is opened, and new levels with higher energy will contribute in current and the effect of interaction is vanished in higher voltages. As a result, the normalized photo-current has a main peak, which is generally increased by the interaction coupling constant α . In the higher voltages the normalized photo-current tends to 1 due to the unaffected states in high energy levels. The
I
P h
/
I
D
> 1 shows the photon-assisted transport through the T-GQD which was investigated for graphene [30–35] and other nano-structures [36–40]. Due to the localized magnetic moments in the zigzag edge of T-GQD [20,22–24], the current through the dot is intrinsically spin-dependent. It should be noted that the polarization does not come from the magnetic electrodes or impurities. The spin-polarization (SP) which is defined as S P = (
I
↑
−
I
↓
) / (
I
↑
+
I
↓
) is plotted in Fig. 5. The SP shows the spin-dependent nature of transport through T-GQD and the amount of SP reveals the splitting between the electronic structure of electrons with spin up and down. The presence of dephasing due to inelastic interaction of electrons change the transmission spectrum of nano-devices [29,41]. Also, we expect the spin-flip scattering and spin dephasing source will decrease the SP in the T-GQD. In the high voltage region the participation of high energy levels in the transport which are not affected by the electron–electron interaction decreases the SP. The negative value of SP in a certain voltage interval indicates that the dominant current is related to electrons with spin down. There are two distinct behavior related to dot with even N = 7 , 11 , 15 and odd N = 9 , 13 number of atoms. In the case of odd structures by increasing the α , SP is mainly decreased after the critical value for interaction constant
α
T
. But, in other structures above the critical interaction strength
α
T
, due to spin independent electronic structure, the SP is equal to zero which corresponds to the spin independent transport. The importance of coupling strength between dot and electrodes in the transport properties of nano structures has been shown in previous works [5,10–13]. Here, by increasing the coupling the effect of electron–photon is screened and SP decreases. Although the SP is maximum in the low voltages but the current is not large enough to detect. The variation of SP as a function of applied voltage leads us to an appropriate work-voltage for future spintronic devices. It was shown that an external electric field controls magnetic moment and SP in the T-GQD [20,24]. To study the normalized photocurrent as a function the incident light energy we consider the photon number dependent part of α as a constant γ (
N
p h
) = 1 / 2 . The resulted normalized photocurrent as a function photon wavelength in different bias voltage is plotted in Fig. 6. The results show a peak in the photocurrent in a certain wavelength which is reported for photocurrent in other nano structures experimentally [42–47]. There is a discontinuity in the normalized current which is related to the phase transition after the critical α. Our results show how to tune net magnetization by using an external photon radiation in a T-GQD. Also, we can determine an appropriate work voltage, and external photon wavelength to gain the best photocurrent in a graphene quantum dot.

In summary, we consider a T-GQD between two infinite electrodes in the presence of photon illumination. The electron-electron interaction in the dot is discussed in the mean-field Hubbard Hamiltonian, and the electron–photon interaction is simplified by means of a unitary-transformation. The effective Hamiltonian is solved self-consistently and the non-equilibrium Green's function is used to calculate current through the nano device. Our results show how to tune the net magnetization and spin polarized current in the T-GQD by using the intensity of the incident light. The present results lead to future photo-detector and spintronic devices based on the graphene nano structures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Prof. B. S. Kandemir and Dr. PawelPotasz for valuable discussions.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Datta, Electronic Transport in Mesoscopic Systems, Cambridge University Press, 3 (1997) 390. ISBN-10: 0521599431, ISBN-13: 978-0521599436
